text.text: It is Not Permissible to Follow This Person

text:
Shaykh al-Islām Ibn Taymīyyah رحمه الله said regarding Allāh’s Statement, Most High:

ٱتَّخَذُوٓا۟ أَحْبَارَهُمْ وَرُهْبَـٰنَهُمْ أَرْبَابًا مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ

text.text: “They took their rabbis (scholars) and monks (devout worshippers) as lords besides Allāh,”

text:
[al-Tawbah: 31]:

“These people who took their rabbis and monks as lords – as they obeyed them in permitting what Allāh prohibited and prohibiting what Allāh permitted –

text.text: are of two types

text:
.

“The first is that they know that they changed Allāh’s Religion, following them in this change and thus believing the permissibility of what Allāh prohibited and prohibition of what Allāh permitted in following their leaders

text.text: while knowing that they opposed the Religion of the Rusul (Messengers)
This is kufr (disbelief), and Allāh and His Rasūl have declared this shirk, even if they do not perform ṣalāh to them (i.e., these rabbis and monks) and prostrate to them

text:
. Hence, whoever follows someone else in opposing the Religion while knowing that it is opposition to the Religion and believes in what they say instead of what Allāh and His Rasūl said is a mushrik just like these people.

“The second is that their belief and īmān in [the impermissibility] of prohibiting permitted things and permitting prohibited things is as it should be, however they obeyed them in disobeying Allāh, just as the Muslim does whatever sins he does while believing them to be sins — these people have the ruling of their likes among those who do sins, just as it has been established from the Nabī ﷺ that he said:

إِنَّمَا الطَّاعَةُ فِي المَعْرُوفِ
“

text.text: ‘Obedience is only in goodness,’

text:
[al-Bukhārī (6830) and Muslim (1840)].

“Furthermore, if this person who prohibits that which is permissible and permits that which is prohibited is a mujtahid whose intent is following the Rasūl, however the truth was hidden from him at the same time and he feared Allāh as much as he was able, then Allāh does not punish this person for his mistake.

text.text: In fact, He rewards him for his ijtihād by which he obeyed his Rabb

However, whoever knows that this is a mistake regarding what the Rasūl came with and then follows him in his mistake and deviates from the statement of the Rasūl, then this person has a portion of the shirk which Allāh rebuked, especially if he followed his desires in doing so and aided [this position] with his hand and tongue while knowing that this is in opposition to the Rasūl.

text:
This is shirk, the perpetrator of which deserves to be punished for it.

“

text.text: For this reason, the ʿulamāʾ agreed that if a person knows the truth then it is not permissible for them to blindly follow anyone in opposition to it

text:
, and they only disagreed on whether it is allowed for someone to blindly follow another person when they are able to derive evidence themselves.”

He further said: “As for if a person blindly follows someone instead of someone equal to them merely due to their desires, and they aid them with their hand and tongue without any knowledge that the truth is with them,

text.text: then this is a person of Jāhilīyyah

text: . If the person they follow is correct, their action is not upright, and if the person they follow is incorrect, they are a sinner, just like a person who speaks regarding the Qurʾān according to their opinion – if they are correct, they have made a mistake, and if they are incorrect,

text.text: then they shall take their place in the Fire

text:
.”

Majmūʿ al-Fatāwā (vol. 7, pgs. 70-71)

Tr: Abū Hurayrah al-Birīṭānī

text.text: To the sister who feels strange in her jilbāb

text:
Know, my beloved sister in īmān, that you will always feel like a gharībah (stranger).

Know that by following the commandments of Allāh and His Messenger ﷺ, you will be faced with insults and mockery.

Know that many will look at your garments with hatred in their hearts. 

Know that the majority will never be pleased with you.

But know that the glad tidings are for the

text.text: ghurabā

text: ʾ.

text.text:
لَتُبْلَوْنَ فِي أَمْوَلِكُمْ وَأَنفُسِكُمْ وَلَتَسْمَعُنَّ مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتُبَ مِن قَبْلِكُمْ وَمِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُواْ أَذَى كَثِيرًا وَإِن تَصْبِرُواْ وَتَتَّقُواْ فَإِنَّ ذَلِكَ مِنْ عَزْمِ الْأَمُورِ

You shall certainly be tried and tested in your wealth and properties and in your personal selves, and you shall certainly hear much that will grieve you from those who received the Scripture before you (Jews and Christians) and from those who ascribe partners to Allāh; but if you persevere patiently, and become Al-Muttaqūn (the pious) then verily, that will be a determining factor in all affairs (and that is from the great matters which you must hold 
on with all your efforts).

– [Sūrah Āli ʿImrān: 186]

text:
We are here to be tested. We are here to be tried. Bear these tests with patience as the righteous before us did.

Let them speak.
Let them mock.
Let them ridicule.

Your value is not in their approval. Your worth is not measured by the amount of praise you receive, how many compliments you get, or how much the world approves of you. 

Your value is with Allāh and obeying Him ﷻ. 

You are wearing the garments of the righteous — the clothing of dignity — the attire of those who chase the ākhirah. 

So let not your heart long for the praise of those who chase the adornments of this dunyā.

Dress for His pleasure, seek His approval.
The only approval you need is His.

For you will find your reward with Allāh, and He is ash-Shakūr (the Most Appreciative).

Jannah is for the people who are strange. Those who enjoin others in righteousness and piety. Those who correct what the people have corrupted of the Sunnah. Those who remain steadfast on this path despite the hardship they face.

Hold firmly to your dīn dear sisters. Hold firmly to the Sunnah. Hold firmly to the way of our salaf (pious predecessors). 

For indeed, blessed are the strangers.

The Prophet ﷺ said:

text.text:
"بَدَأَ الإِسْلاَمُ غَرِيبًا وَسَيَعُودُ كَمَا بَدَأَ غَرِيبًا فَطُوبَى لِلْغُرَبَاءِ‏"
“Islam began as something strange and it will return to being strange, so glad tidings to the strangers.”
[Ṣaḥīḥ Muslim 145]

text: - أم مصعب الغانية الألمانية

Let them mock.
Let them ridicule.

Your value is not in their approval. Your worth is not measured by the amount of praise you receive, how many compliments you get, or how much the world approves of you. 

Your value is with Allāh and obeying Him ﷻ. 

You are wearing the garments of the righteous — the clothing of dignity — the attire of those who chase the ākhirah. 

So let not your heart long for the praise of those who chase the adornments of this dunyā.

Dress for His pleasure, seek His approval.
The only approval you need is His.

For you will find your reward with Allāh, and He is ash-Shakūr (the Most Appreciative).

Jannah is for the people who are strange. Those who enjoin others in righteousness and piety. Those who correct what the people have corrupted of the Sunnah. Those who remain steadfast on this path despite the hardship they face.

Hold firmly to your dīn dear sisters. Hold firmly to the Sunnah. Hold firmly to the way of our salaf (pious predecessors). 

For indeed, blessed are the strangers.

The Prophet ﷺ said:

"بَدَأَ الإِسْلاَمُ غَرِيبًا وَسَيَعُودُ كَمَا بَدَأَ غَرِيبًا فَطُوبَى لِلْغُرَبَاءِ‏"
“Islam began as something strange and it will return to being strange, so glad tidings to the strangers.”
[Ṣaḥīḥ Muslim 145]
- أم مصعب الغانية الألمانية

text:
Shaykh Ṣāliḥ al-Fawzān حفظه الله said:

"You will be a stranger among people, so hold firmly to your religion even if most people go astray. Even if you become a stranger among people, be patient with this. As long as you are upon the truth, it should not concern you. It is not upon you to please so-and-so, nor does it concern you if so-and-so is upset—what is upon you is this religion. This requires patience, because people may talk about you, or perhaps strike you, or perhaps imprison you for your religion, but be patient with your religion. Be patient.

text.text: "bear with patience whatever befalls you." [Luqmān: 17]

text:
"

[Sharḥ Kitāb al-Fitan wal-Ḥawādith, Lesson 11]

text.text: Tr: Umm ʿAbdirraḥmān as-Sūdāniyyah

text.text: 🌱 Understanding the Nature of Children

text:
When you understand the nature of children, many things that would normally cause frustration become easier to bear with patience and wisdom.

A young child — from birth to usually around four years old — naturally sees the world as revolving around them, everything belongs to them in their head. They may take things that don’t belong to them or refuse to share, not out of bad intention, rather it's their nature, they haven’t yet developed the concept of ownership or the awareness of others — they'll eventually grow out of it if you teach them well with gentleness.  

So instead of reacting with anger, you respond by teaching them with patience and ḥilm (forbearance). They are not disobeying out of bad intention, but lack of understanding. 

Similarly, younger children are naturally drawn to what they can see and experience more than what they are told.

If, for example, an eight year old child delays prayer or prefers playing games over ḥifẓ, it's not because they hate worship or reject obedience to Allāh, rather, it is because:

The ghayb (unseen) is still abstract to them, while the dunya is immediate and tangible. So they don't want to "miss out" on the fun while praying or memorising.

And this is something even adults struggle with, let alone children. 

So tarbiyah here requires:

• Gradual nurturing of īmān
• Patience over years, not days
• Consistency without harshness

You plant the seed early, but you don’t expect the tree to grow overnight.

This also extends beyond children. 

When you understand people's nature, you begin to deal with them with wisdom, not mere reactions.

• A man who understands emotional fluctuations may show more patience and sympathy instead of taking things personally.

• A woman who understands that anger is a human nature Allāh trialed men with especially may respond with more restraint instead of provocatively.

So you deal with people with

text.text: their

text:
level of understanding — not your own.

With that said, this understanding is an excuse for others, but

text.text: not a justification for ourselves.

text:
You assume the best of your Muslim brothers and sisters, you excuse them, you show mercy. 

But you also hold yourself accountable, you strive against the blameworthy traits you were tested with, you don’t hide behind “this is just my nature”.

text.text:
- كتبته أم عبدالرحمن السودانية،
٦ شوال ١٤٧٧هـ

their

level of understanding — not your own.

text.text: The Head Is the Image

text:
My sisters in Islam:

Being lenient regarding images and picture-making is extremely dangerous. It is one of the major sins. It involves being cursed, and it prevents angels from entering, which in turn allows devils to take over.

text.text: What is the ruling on these images? And what must we avoid?

text: Images of living beings are prohibited when the head is present, based on the Prophet’s statement ﷺ:

text.text: «الصُّورَةُ الرَّأسُ، فَإِذَا قُطِعَ الرَّأسُ فلَا صُورَةَ»
"The image is the head; if the head is cut off, there is no image."

text:
[As-Sahihah, 1921] 

And his statement ﷺ:

text.text: «فَمُرْ بِرَأْسِ التِّمْثَالِ الَّذِي فِي الْبَيْتِ يُقْطَعُ، فَيَصِيرُ كَهَيْئَةِ الشَّجَرَةِ»
"Command that the head of the statue in the house be cut off, so it becomes like the shape of a tree."

text:
[Narrated by Abu Dawud, 4158, from Abu Hurayrah, may Allah be pleased with him - graded 'Sahih' by Sheikh Albani] 

The head is well-known.

text.text: Blurring the eyes or the face does not suffice as an alternative to cutting off the head
Whether the head is facing forward or backward, and whether the facial features are visible or not—it is still a head.

text:
Once the head is removed, we are no longer in violation. The image is (essentially) the head! 

As for the remaining body parts of living beings, scholars have differed. The more cautious approach is to avoid them unless there is a need. However, this applies only if the body parts are NOT THOSE OF A WOMAN. It is not permissible to display a woman’s body parts, neither in reality nor in images, based on the Prophet’s statement ﷺ:

text.text: «المَرْأَةُ عَوْرَةٌ»
"A woman is ‘awrah (to be covered)."

text:
[Narrated by At-Tirmidhi, 1173, from Ibn Mas‘ud, may Allah be pleased with him)] 

A woman is commanded to cover herself and conceal her adornment. Her entire body is to be covered. So what about those who display women’s hands decorated with henna? This is a source of fitnah.

And Allah is the One whose help is sought.

✍🏻 Umm Hafs Al-Hadhramiyyah Al-‘Adaniyyah, daughter of ‘Umar ibn Silm

Source:

text.text: https://t.me/alhadramiyaal3adaniya/7297

messages.from: عَلَىٰ نَهجِ السَّلَفِ || Upon the path of the Salaf 📚

text.text: How to Achieve Sincerity When Seeking Knowledge

text: ʾImām Aḥmad -may Allāh have mercy on him- said:

text.text: «Knowledge is equaled by nothing for the one whose intention is sound.»

text: They asked: "And how does one's intention become sound, O Abā ʿAbdillāh?"  He replied:

text.text: «He intends to remove ignorance from himself and from others.»

text:
If one asks: How is sincerity achieved in seeking knowledge?

We say: Sincerity in seeking knowledge is achieved by intending the following matters:

• The First Matter: Fulfilling the command of Allāh, as Allāh the Exalted has commanded it. He said:

text.text: "So know (O Muḥammad ﷺ) that, Lā ilāha illallāh (none has the right to be worshipped but Allāh), and ask forgiveness for your sin..” [Sūrat Muḥammad: 19]

text:
And He—Glorified and Exalted is He—encouraged knowledge. Encouraging something necessitates loving it, being pleased with it, and commanding it.

• The Second Matter: Preserving the Shari'ah of Allāh, as it is achieved through learning, memorization in the chest, and through writing.

• The Third Matter: Protecting the Shariʿah and defending it. For were it not for the scholars, the Shariʿah would not be protected, nor would anyone defend it. We see, for example, Shaykh al-ʾIslām Ibn Taymiyyah and other scholars who faced the people of innovation and exposed the falsehood of their innovations. We can see they obtained abundant good.

• The Fourth Matter: Following the Shariʿah of Muḥammad ﷺ, for you cannot follow his Shariʿah unless you know it.

These are four matters, all of which are encompassed by our statement:

text.text: Sincerity to Allāh is obligatory in seeking knowledge.

text:
Sharḥ Ḥilyat Ṭālib al-ʿIlm (p.16-17)
Tr: Umm Tamīm aṣ-Ṣūmāliyyah

«Knowledge is equaled by nothing for the one whose intention is sound.»

«He intends to remove ignorance from himself and from others.»

If one asks: How is sincerity achieved in seeking knowledge?

We say: Sincerity in seeking knowledge is achieved by intending the following matters:

• The First Matter: Fulfilling the command of Allāh, as Allāh the Exalted has commanded it. He said:
"So know (O Muḥammad ﷺ) that, Lā ilāha illallāh (none has the right to be worshipped but Allāh), and ask forgiveness for your sin..” [Sūrat Muḥammad: 19]

And He—Glorified and Exalted is He—encouraged knowledge. Encouraging something necessitates loving it, being pleased with it, and commanding it.

• The Second Matter: Preserving the Shari'ah of Allāh, as it is achieved through learning, memorization in the chest, and through writing.

• The Third Matter: Protecting the Shariʿah and defending it. For were it not for the scholars, the Shariʿah would not be protected, nor would anyone defend it. We see, for example, Shaykh al-ʾIslām Ibn Taymiyyah and other scholars who faced the people of innovation and exposed the falsehood of their innovations. We can see they obtained abundant good.

• The Fourth Matter: Following the Shariʿah of Muḥammad ﷺ, for you cannot follow his Shariʿah unless you know it.

These are four matters, all of which are encompassed by our statement:
Sincerity to Allāh is obligatory in seeking knowledge.

Sharḥ Ḥilyat Ṭālib al-ʿIlm (p.16-17)
Tr: Umm Tamīm aṣ-Ṣūmāliyyah

text.text: Women of the Salaf || نِسَاءُ السَّلَف

🌸 Shaykha Umm ʿAbdillāh mentioned her father’s (Shaykh Muqbil) advice to Women:

It is upon the women to study the biography of the Prophet Muḥammad ﷺ and the biographies of the female companions who lived during the Prophet's time, such as Umm Sulaym and other virtuous women of the Companions.

Follow their example if you hope to attain Allāh's pleasure and the Hereafter.

 [مقدمة والدي لـ نصيحتي للنساء].
Umm Zaynab Fāṭimah al-Baghdādiyyah
Umm Kulthūm bint ʿUqbah ibn Abī Muʿayṭ
Khadījah bint Khuwaylid

text.text: The Noble Characteristics of Ibn al-Qayyim

text:
Ibn Kathīr رحمه الله said in describing Ibn al-Qayyim رحمه الله:

“He had good recitation and manners and had much love for others, and he did not envy, harm, or search for the faults of anyone, nor would he despise anyone. I was among those who accompanied him the most and the most beloved of people to him, and I do not know anyone among the people of knowledge in our time who did more worship than him. He had a method in the ṣalāh which he would lengthen greatly, and he would extend its rukūʿ and sujūd. Many of his companions would rebuke him [for this] at times, but he would not stop or leave off doing so, may Allāh have mercy on him.”

Al-Bidāyah wa al-Nihāyah (vol. 18, pg. 523)

Benefitted from the book ‘Ḥasad: Its Types, Harms, Signs, and Cure’ by Abū ʿAbd al-Raḥmān Nūr al-Dīn ibn Aḥmad al-Ḥubayshī

Tr: Abū Hurayrah al-Birīṭānī

Join Channel:

text.text: https://t.me/fawaid_benefits

text.text: A nice benefit taken from today’s dars on ʿUmdatul Aḥkām by Umm ʿAbdillāh Shahīdah حفظها الله
At-tabarruj

text:
: كثرة الخروج بغير حاجة — it is women leaving the house a lot without necessity (adorning yourself with improper clothing is also included in the definition).

وَقَرْنَ فِى بُيُوتِكُنَّ وَلَا تَبَرَّجْنَ تَبَرُّجَ ٱلْجَـٰهِلِيَّةِ ٱلْأُولَىٰ

text.text: “And stay in your houses, and do not display yourselves like that of the times of ignorance”

text: Ibn Kathīr commented on the āyah

text.text: [Sūrah Al-Aḥzāb:33]

text:
with this definition, as said by the Tābiʿī Mujāhid ibn Jabr. It is also defined as this in the books of لغة.

This phrase is now generally not used with its original meaning. When one says ‘tabarruj’ or ‘mutabarrijah’ they mean a woman who wears incorrect jilbāb or displays her adornments. However, the aṣl of tabarruj is frequently leaving the home without need.

Therefore, even if she is covered properly with the sharʿī clothing but she is out and about everyday (without necessity), then

text.text: this is at-tabarruj.

text: What is worse is when she’s out of the house a lot

text.text: and

text:
not covered properly.

Leaving the house everyday (even if you are wearing the correct jilbāb) is a fitnah.

The wives of the shuyūkh do not go out. They may only go to the masjid and then come straight back. This is due to their baṣīrah—they fully understand the commands of Allāh ﷻ and what it leads to when they are not upheld.

The Shaykha mentioned that she saw Sheikh Muqbil’s wife only once, on ʿeid, however she did not stay long and had to return back home soon after. In regards to Sheikh Yaḥyā’s wives: “you don’t see his wives” and this is how it is with the other shuyūkh.

والله المستعان

- أم مصعب الغانية الألمانية

text_entities.text: A nice benefit taken from today’s dars on ʿUmdatul Aḥkām by Umm ʿAbdillāh Shahīdah حفظها الله
At-tabarruj

: كثرة الخروج بغير حاجة — it is women leaving the house a lot without necessity (adorning yourself with improper clothing is also included in the definition).

وَقَرْنَ فِى بُيُوتِكُنَّ وَلَا تَبَرَّجْنَ تَبَرُّجَ ٱلْجَـٰهِلِيَّةِ ٱلْأُولَىٰ
“And stay in your houses, and do not display yourselves like that of the times of ignorance”
Ibn Kathīr commented on the āyah
[Sūrah Al-Aḥzāb:33]

with this definition, as said by the Tābiʿī Mujāhid ibn Jabr. It is also defined as this in the books of لغة.

This phrase is now generally not used with its original meaning. When one says ‘tabarruj’ or ‘mutabarrijah’ they mean a woman who wears incorrect jilbāb or displays her adornments. However, the aṣl of tabarruj is frequently leaving the home without need.

Therefore, even if she is covered properly with the sharʿī clothing but she is out and about everyday (without necessity), then
this is at-tabarruj.
What is worse is when she’s out of the house a lot
and

not covered properly.

Leaving the house everyday (even if you are wearing the correct jilbāb) is a fitnah.

The wives of the shuyūkh do not go out. They may only go to the masjid and then come straight back. This is due to their baṣīrah—they fully understand the commands of Allāh ﷻ and what it leads to when they are not upheld.

The Shaykha mentioned that she saw Sheikh Muqbil’s wife only once, on ʿeid, however she did not stay long and had to return back home soon after. In regards to Sheikh Yaḥyā’s wives: “you don’t see his wives” and this is how it is with the other shuyūkh.

والله المستعان

- أم مصعب الغانية الألمانية

messages.from: عَلَىٰ نَهجِ السَّلَفِ || Upon the path of the Salaf 📚

text.text: Point from yesterday’s lesson: women not leaving the house unnecessarily, except for a genuine need

text: By: Shaykha Umm

ʿAbdillāh Shahīdah Al-Briṭāniyyah
——•——

The ḥadīth:

text.text: «إن المرأة عورة، فإذا خرجت استشرفها الشيطان»
“The woman is ʿawrah, so when she leaves the house, Shayṭān seeks to draw attention to her [make a big deal out of her].”
Meaning:

text:
her presence becomes noticed, and attention may be drawn towards her.
 • This attention can affect a person over time, possibly changing her demeanour if she becomes used to being outside frequently.
 • Shayṭān may then follow this with

text.text: waswas

text:
(whispers), influencing her thoughts and intentions.

As you grow older, you realise that your place, your happiness is really at home. And if you have a good family, it is a home with your husband and children—whom you find to be your closest friends. They are the people who are always there for you and want to protect you no matter what.

Our ustādha reflected on the āyah:

text.text: «وقرن في بيوتكن»

text: She explained that this part of the āyah clarifies the meaning of:

text.text: «ولا تبرجن تبرج الجاهلية الأولى»

text: In other words, remaining in the home is part of what defines avoiding

text.text: tabarruj

text:
.

In a society where women are encouraged to constantly be outside and active in public spaces, this concept may feel unfamiliar. However, this was also the case before Islam — among previous communities such as the Jews and Christians.

- أم مصعب الغانية الألمانية

By: Shaykha Umm

ʿAbdillāh Shahīdah Al-Briṭāniyyah

Meaning:

her presence becomes noticed, and attention may be drawn towards her.
 • This attention can affect a person over time, possibly changing her demeanour if she becomes used to being outside frequently.
 • Shayṭān may then follow this with
waswas

(whispers), influencing her thoughts and intentions.

As you grow older, you realise that your place, your happiness is really at home. And if you have a good family, it is a home with your husband and children—whom you find to be your closest friends. They are the people who are always there for you and want to protect you no matter what.

Our ustādha reflected on the āyah:
«وقرن في بيوتكن»

tabarruj

.

messages.from: عَلَىٰ نَهجِ السَّلَفِ || Upon the path of the Salaf 📚

text.text: It is mentioned by Al-Qurṭubī in his tafsīr, regarding
the āyah “and remain in your homes” [33:33]:

ذكر الثعلبيّ وغيره: أن عائشة
- رضي اللّٰه عنها - كانت إذا قرأت هذه الآية تبكي حتى تَبُلّ خمارها. وذكر أن سَوْدة قيل لها: لم لا تحجّين ولا تَعْتَمرين كما يفعل أخواتك؟ فقالت:
قد حججت واعتمرت، وأمرني اللّٰه أن أقرّ في بيتي. قال الراوي: فوالله ما خرجت من باب حجرتها حتى أخرجت جنازتها. رضوان لله عليها!

text:
Al-Thaʿlabī and others mention that whenever ʿĀʾishah رضي الله عنها recited this āyah, she would cry until her veil became wet.

And it was mentioned that Sawdah رضي الله عنها said to her:

“Why do you not perform Ḥajj or ʿumrah as your sisters do?”

ʿĀʾishah replied: “Indeed I have performed Ḥajj and ʿumrah and

text.text: Allāh has ordered me to stay in my house

text:
.”

It is also reported, “And by Allāh, she did not go out of her house until her coffin was brought out.”

May Allāh be pleased with her.

text.text: تفسير القرطبي ١٧/١٤٣

text.text: From what has been said about brotherhood for the sake of Allāh:

text:
"The example of two brothers: when they meet, they are like two hands washing one another."

Shaykh al-Islām said:
“Indeed the believer to another believer is like two hands washing one another.”
(Majmūʿ al-Fatāwā 28/53)

text.text: From what has been said about brotherhood for the sake of Allāh:

text: “The example of brotherhood for Allāh’s sake is like the hand and the eye: If the eye sheds tears, the hand wipes them; and if the hand is in pain, the eye weeps for it.”

text.text: From what has been said about brotherhood:

text:
“The soul has never attained a desire
more delightful than the affection of a trustworthy friend.
Whoever is deprived of the love of a righteous brother —
it is he who's aorta has been cut.”

forwarded from:

text.text: Shaykh Abū ʿĀmir ʿAbdullāh al-Ḥakami
Tr: Umm ʿAbdirraḥmān as-Sūdāniyyah

text: Bakr ibn ‘Abdullāh al-Muzanī (رحمه الله) said:

text.text: إياك من الكلام ما إن أصبت فيه لم تؤجر، وإن أخطأت فيه أثمت، وهو سوء الظن بأخيك!

text:
“Beware of speech such that, if you are correct in it, you are not rewarded, and if you are mistaken in it, you incur sin. That is having bad thoughts about your brother.”

Tahdhīb at-Tahdhīb (1/484)

text.text: There are 3 levels of forbidding evil (munkar) from the ḥadīth of the Messenger of Allāh ﷺ:

"Whoever amongst you sees an evil, he must change it with his hand; if he is unable to do so, then with his tongue; and if he is unable to do so, then with his heart; and that is the weakest form of ʾImān." Ṣaḥīḥ Muslim (49).
(1) Forbidding evil with the hand.

text: This is the strongest level, such as: Removing wine, breaking idols as the Prophet ʾIbrāhīm عليه السلام did, as well as with the Prophet ﷺ removing a gold ring from a man's hand.

text.text: (2) Forbidding evil with the tongue.

text: This is done with wisdom and gentleness, unless the situation calls for harshness and severity. If one fears that this may lead to a greater evil, then he should move to the next level. As Shaykh al-ʾIslām Ibn Taymiyyah said: "No one has the right to remove an evil with something that is more evil than it." Kitāb al-Mustadrak ʿalā Majmūʿ al-Fatāwā (3/203).

text.text: (3) Forbidding evil with the heart.

text:
This means to hate the munkar, distance oneself from it, and hate the people of it. This is obligatory and never falls away from the believer. It is the weakest of ʾĪmān, as mentioned by our beloved Prophet ﷺ.

Note: Whenever you fear harm befalling you, move to the next level.

📍 Benefit:

text.text: Should I advise even if I know they will not accept it?

text:
Shaykh al-ʾIslām Ibn Taymiyyah (may Allāh have mercy on him) said in Kitāb al-Mustadrak ʿalā Majmūʿ al-Fatāwā (3/203-204): "If a man strongly believes that his enjoining good and forbidding evil will not be obeyed, is it still obligatory upon him? There are two opinions. The more correct of them is that it is obligatory, even if it is not accepted from him, as long as the evil of abandoning it does not outweigh the evil of doing it. This is like Nūḥ عليه السلام who remained calling his people for a thousand years minus fifty years." (End of quote)

Advice: Do not belittle nor take lightly the matter of enjoining good and forbidding evil. Let us pay attention to this ḥadīth and act upon it. Let our advice be sincerely for the sake of Allāh, so that it may be accepted and bring about righteousness.

The Prophet ﷺ said:

text.text: "Let none of you belittle himself. If he sees a matter concerning Allāh for which he should speak, and he does not speak, it will be said to him [on the Day of Resurrection]: 'What prevented you?' He will say: 'Fear of the people.' Then He (Allāh ﷻ) will say: 'Rather, it was Me you should have feared.'" Ibn Mājah (4008).

text: How can we fear a mere rude glance or the loss of companionship? Do we not fear Allāh? By Allāh, this ḥadīth makes a person realize how much they have fallen short in this matter. Indeed,

text.text: it is Allāh we should fear.

text: Ibn al-Qayyim (may Allāh have mercy on him) said in his book Ṭarīq al-Hijratayn wa Bāb as-Saʿādatayn (p. 63):

text.text: "The truly happy and successful one is he who deals with Allāh concerning the people, and does not deal with the people concerning Allāh; who fears Allāh with regard to them, and does not fear them with regard to Allāh; who pleases Allāh even if it angers them, and does not please them at the cost of Allāh’s displeasure; who observes Allāh concerning them, and does not observe them concerning Allāh; who gives precedence to Allah over them, and does not give them precedence over Allāh..”

text:
This is how we ought to be! Do not worry about people turning away from you or being angry with you, as long as you are upon the Qurʾān and the Sunnah. Be gentle with the common people, be a sincere advisor, be close to them, make the Sunnah beloved to them, and make innovations hated to them.

Allāh ﷻ said:

text.text: “Invite (mankind, O Muhammad صلى الله عليه و سلم) to the Way of your Lord (i.e. Islâm) with wisdom (i.e. with the Divine Revelation and the Qur’ân) and fair preaching, and argue with them in a way that is better..” [Sūrat an-Naḥl: 125]

text:
May Allāh make us among His sincere servants who call to the Sunnah of the Prophet ﷺ.

Benefitted from the explanation of "Al-Ḥulal az-Zāhirah" by our Shaykha may Allāh preserve her.
—Umm Tamīm aṣ-Ṣūmāliyyah

it is Allāh we should fear.
Ibn al-Qayyim (may Allāh have mercy on him) said in his book Ṭarīq al-Hijratayn wa Bāb as-Saʿādatayn (p. 63):

Allāh ﷻ said:

“Invite (mankind, O Muhammad صلى الله عليه و سلم) to the Way of your Lord (i.e. Islâm) with wisdom (i.e. with the Divine Revelation and the Qur’ân) and fair preaching, and argue with them in a way that is better..” [Sūrat an-Naḥl: 125]

May Allāh make us among His sincere servants who call to the Sunnah of the Prophet ﷺ.

Benefitted from the explanation of "Al-Ḥulal az-Zāhirah" by our Shaykha may Allāh preserve her.
—Umm Tamīm aṣ-Ṣūmāliyyah

text.text:
A student of Ibn Taymiyyah whom he would prepare answers for:
Umm Zaynab Fāṭimah al-Baghdādiyyah (born 640 AH – died 724 AH)
Ibn Kathīr رحمه الله said:

text:
“The righteous, devout, and worshipful Shaykha — Umm Zaynab Fāṭimah bint ʿAbbās ibn Abī al-Fatḥ ibn Muḥammad al-Baghdādiyyah — was among the virtuous and knowledgeable women.

She enjoined good and forbade evil, and she would confront the Aḥmadiyyah (a deviant sect) regarding their practice of forming friendships with women and handsome youths, and she would condemn their states and the states of other innovators. She used to do in this regard what many men were unable to do.

She would attend the gatherings of Shaykh Taqiyy ad-Dīn Ibn Taymiyyah, from whom she benefited in that and other matters.

I heard Shaykh Taqiyy ad-Dīn praise her, describing her as virtuous and knowledgeable, and mentioning that she had memorized much — or most — of al-Mughnī, and that he would prepare himself for her because of the abundance of her questions, the excellence of her inquiries, and her quick understanding.

She was the one who supervised the completion of Qur’ān memorization for many women — among them the mother of my wife, ʿĀ’ishah bint Ṣiddīq, the wife of Shaykh Jamāl ad-Dīn al-Mizzī — and she was also the one who taught their daughter, my wife, Amat ar-Raḥīm Zaynab.

May Allāh have mercy on them and honor them with His mercy and His Paradise. Āmīn.”

text.text: Al-Bidāyah wa an-Nihāyah (vol. 18, p. 140)

text: Tr: Umm ʿAbdirraḥmān as-Sūdāniyyah

____

She used to do in this regard what many men were unable to do.

She would attend the gatherings of Shaykh Taqiyy ad-Dīn Ibn Taymiyyah, from whom she benefited in that and other matters.

I heard Shaykh Taqiyy ad-Dīn praise her, describing her as virtuous and knowledgeable, and mentioning that she had memorized much — or most — of al-Mughnī, and that he would prepare himself for her because of the abundance of her questions, the excellence of her inquiries, and her quick understanding.

She was the one who supervised the completion of Qur’ān memorization for many women — among them the mother of my wife, ʿĀ’ishah bint Ṣiddīq, the wife of Shaykh Jamāl ad-Dīn al-Mizzī — and she was also the one who taught their daughter, my wife, Amat ar-Raḥīm Zaynab.

May Allāh have mercy on them and honor them with His mercy and His Paradise. Āmīn.”
Al-Bidāyah wa an-Nihāyah (vol. 18, p. 140)
Tr: Umm ʿAbdirraḥmān as-Sūdāniyyah

text.text:
Important Reminder on the Matter of Note-Taking
———•———•———•———•———

text: In the most recent lesson of al-Mabādī al-Mufīdah by Ustādh Abū Hurayrah Al-Brīṭānī, the issue of note-taking and sharing notes was discussed. Ustādh noted mistakes or things that could be better.

text.text: So, how does a person take good notes?

text: The

text.text: aṣl

text:
is that it is not done digitally, but it is done by pen and paper. If one wishes, he can write them up digitally after writing by hand. This is khair—no problem in this.

One benefit of this: taking notes by writing physically is a means for a person to memorise and remember [the content] very well. As your mind is 100% engaged in what you’re doing, it becomes ingrained. The phone/computer is not the same.

text.text: What does a person write?

text: The aṣl is that he should write every single word the teacher says. Why? Because the teacher says the words he says

text.text: for a reason

text: and he chooses the words carefully and says it with a purpose.

text.text: Every single word is important!

text:
If they are able, they should strive to write every single word.

Of course, if the teacher repeats something to make it clear and allow it to stay in your mind and to make sure it’s understood, this is a chance to catch up what’s missed—you don't need to write it again.

Pay attention to the words. Don’t change words.
This can inadvertently change the meaning.

For example, if the teacher says something has been “narrated from Prophet ﷺ,” and in their notes they say “the Prophet ﷺ said this…” —but this is

text.text: not

text:
what Ustādh has said.

There is a difference between these two things. When the Ustādh said this [the former], he is weakening this narration or indicating it may not be ṣaḥīḥ. However, if one writes the latter in their notes, they are attributing to the teacher

text.text: something he did not say

text:
.

If they [the teacher] say “in my opinion..” or “Allāh knows best, this is the correct position..” and they write “the correct opinion is..”, this is not right. The former wording is indicating the fact that the teacher could be incorrect.

text.text: Therefore,

text:
•Pay close attention to wordings used and the intent, especially if it’s being published or shared with other people.
•If a person misses something, then just leave a blank space. If you fill in the gaps [yourself], you may well attribute something to the teacher that they didn’t say. 
•If there’s a recording, then fill in gaps later, if not then compare notes with others. If you try fill in gaps, you may be incorrect.

Strive to write

text.text: everything

text:
a teacher has said!

After the lesson, one can summarise notes in a way that makes it easy to revise.

🖊️Umm Muṣʿab Al-Ghāniyyah Al-Almāniyyah

text_entities.text:
Important Reminder on the Matter of Note-Taking
———•———•———•———•———
In the most recent lesson of al-Mabādī al-Mufīdah by Ustādh Abū Hurayrah Al-Brīṭānī, the issue of note-taking and sharing notes was discussed. Ustādh noted mistakes or things that could be better.
So, how does a person take good notes?
The
aṣl

is that it is not done digitally, but it is done by pen and paper. If one wishes, he can write them up digitally after writing by hand. This is khair—no problem in this.

One benefit of this: taking notes by writing physically is a means for a person to memorise and remember [the content] very well. As your mind is 100% engaged in what you’re doing, it becomes ingrained. The phone/computer is not the same.
What does a person write?
The aṣl is that he should write every single word the teacher says. Why? Because the teacher says the words he says
for a reason
and he chooses the words carefully and says it with a purpose.
Every single word is important!

If they are able, they should strive to write every single word.

Of course, if the teacher repeats something to make it clear and allow it to stay in your mind and to make sure it’s understood, this is a chance to catch up what’s missed—you don't need to write it again.

Pay attention to the words. Don’t change words.
This can inadvertently change the meaning.

For example, if the teacher says something has been “narrated from Prophet ﷺ,” and in their notes they say “the Prophet ﷺ said this…” —but this is
not

what Ustādh has said.

There is a difference between these two things. When the Ustādh said this [the former], he is weakening this narration or indicating it may not be ṣaḥīḥ. However, if one writes the latter in their notes, they are attributing to the teacher
something he did not say

.

If they [the teacher] say “in my opinion..” or “Allāh knows best, this is the correct position..” and they write “the correct opinion is..”, this is not right. The former wording is indicating the fact that the teacher could be incorrect.
Therefore,

•Pay close attention to wordings used and the intent, especially if it’s being published or shared with other people.
•If a person misses something, then just leave a blank space. If you fill in the gaps [yourself], you may well attribute something to the teacher that they didn’t say. 
•If there’s a recording, then fill in gaps later, if not then compare notes with others. If you try fill in gaps, you may be incorrect.

Strive to write
everything

a teacher has said!

After the lesson, one can summarise notes in a way that makes it easy to revise.

🖊️Umm Muṣʿab Al-Ghāniyyah Al-Almāniyyah

messages.from: عَلَىٰ نَهجِ السَّلَفِ || Upon the path of the Salaf 📚
text:
In a recent class, a sister posed a question to Shaykha Umm ʿAbdillah Al-Brīṭāniyyah regarding note-taking.

Our Shaykha حفظها الله responded and said we should strive and try to make notes. She gives us time so we are able to write down notes; either by pausing or repeating things, or slowing down when translating etc.

She mentioned that, when she was studying in Dammāj, some teachers would give you some time to write down your notes as their pace was slower. However, in Sheikh Yaḥyā’s durūs, “he would

text.text: not

text:
give you time” meaning he spoke very fast in his lessons making it difficult to jot down everything he says. 

Therefore, Shaykha told us that, sometimes she would have blanks and would need to ask another sister for her notes in order to fill in the gaps. She would specifically go to a sister who she knows has memorised the ḥadīth and she is a sister who takes good notes.

However, we should not rely on other people’s notes as they could have mistakes and they may not get everything down.

She also encouraged us to write by hand because it helps us get used to writing in Arabic (that is when our lessons are in Arabic).

🖊️Umm Muṣʿab Al-Ghāniyyah Al-Almāniyyah

Our Shaykha حفظها الله responded and said we should strive and try to make notes. She gives us time so we are able to write down notes; either by pausing or repeating things, or slowing down when translating etc.

She mentioned that, when she was studying in Dammāj, some teachers would give you some time to write down your notes as their pace was slower. However, in Sheikh Yaḥyā’s durūs, “he would
not

give you time” meaning he spoke very fast in his lessons making it difficult to jot down everything he says. 

Therefore, Shaykha told us that, sometimes she would have blanks and would need to ask another sister for her notes in order to fill in the gaps. She would specifically go to a sister who she knows has memorised the ḥadīth and she is a sister who takes good notes.

However, we should not rely on other people’s notes as they could have mistakes and they may not get everything down.

She also encouraged us to write by hand because it helps us get used to writing in Arabic (that is when our lessons are in Arabic).

🖊️Umm Muṣʿab Al-Ghāniyyah Al-Almāniyyah

text.text: The first Muhājirah after the Hijrah of the Prophet ﷺ:
Umm Kulthūm bint ʿUqbah ibn Abī Muʿayṭ

text:
She accepted Islam in Makkah and gave the pledge of allegiance (bayʿah) before the Hijrah. She was the first woman to emmigrate after the Messenger of Allāh ﷺ emmigrated to Madīnah. She emmigrated during the truce of Ḥudaybiyah.

Rabīʿah ibn ʿUthmān and Qudāmah both said: "We do not know of any Qurashī woman who left her parents as a Muslim emigrant except Umm Kulthūm."

She said: "I used to go out to a desert area of ours where my family resided, staying there for three or four nights—it was in the direction of Tanʿīm—then I would return to my family. They did not object to my going to the desert area until I resolved to set out [for Madīnah]. One day I left Makkah as if I intended to go to the desert, and when those who were following me turned back I saw a man from Khuzāʿah. He asked: 'Where do you intend to go?' I said: 'Why do you ask? Who are you?' He said: 'A man from Khuzāʿah.' When he mentioned Khuzāʿah, I felt at ease because the tribe of Khuzāʿah was included in the covenant and allegiance of the Messenger of Allāh ﷺ. So I said: 'I am a woman from Quraysh, and I wish to join the Messenger of Allāh ﷺ, but I do not know the way.'
He said: 'I will be your companion until I bring you to Madīnah.'

Then he brought me a camel and I rode it. He led the camel, and by Allāh, he did not speak a single word to me. When he made the camel kneel, he moved away from me. When I dismounted, he would come to the camel, tie it to a tree, and move away to the shade of another tree. When it was time to depart, he would saddle the camel and lead it away, turning away from me. When I mounted, he would take hold of its head and not look back until I dismounted. He continued like that until we reached Madīnah. May Allāh reward him for his good companionship.

I then entered upon Umm Salamah while I was veiled, she did not recognize me until I introduced myself and uncovered my veil. She embraced me and said: 'You have emmigrated to Allāh, Mighty and Majestic, and to His Messenger?' I said: 'Yes, but I fear that he will send me back just as he sent back Abū Jandal and Abū Baṣīr, and the situation of men is not like that of women. My people will search for me, I have been away for five days now since I left them. They will estimate the amount of time I usually stayed away, then they will search for me. If they do not find me, they will set out.'”

Then the Messenger of Allāh ﷺ entered upon Umm Salamah, and she informed him of the news of Umm Kulthūm. He welcomed and reassured her.

I said: "I have fled to you with my religion. Keep me and do not send me back to them, for they trial and torture me. I have no patience for torture. I am only a woman—weak as women are, as you know. And I have seen that you sent back two men until one of them refused."
He said: "Indeed, Allāh, Mighty and Majestic, has nullified the treaty regarding women and has made a ruling concerning them with which they are all pleased." As he used to send back the women.

The next day, her two brothers—Al-Walīd and ʿAmmārah—came and said: "Fulfill your promise and the covenant you made with us."
He said: "Allāh has nullified the treaty." So they left.
                                                           (1/2)

text_entities.text: The first Muhājirah after the Hijrah of the Prophet ﷺ:
Umm Kulthūm bint ʿUqbah ibn Abī Muʿayṭ

She accepted Islam in Makkah and gave the pledge of allegiance (bayʿah) before the Hijrah. She was the first woman to emmigrate after the Messenger of Allāh ﷺ emmigrated to Madīnah. She emmigrated during the truce of Ḥudaybiyah.

Rabīʿah ibn ʿUthmān and Qudāmah both said: "We do not know of any Qurashī woman who left her parents as a Muslim emigrant except Umm Kulthūm."

They did not object to my going to the desert area until I resolved to set out [for Madīnah]. So I said: 'I am a woman from Quraysh, and I wish to join the Messenger of Allāh ﷺ, but I do not know the way.'
He said: 'I will be your companion until I bring you to Madīnah.'

Then he brought me a camel and I rode it. He led the camel, and by Allāh, he did not speak a single word to me. When he made the camel kneel, he moved away from me. When I dismounted, he would come to the camel, tie it to a tree, and move away to the shade of another tree. When it was time to depart, he would saddle the camel and lead it away, turning away from me. When I mounted, he would take hold of its head and not look back until I dismounted. He continued like that until we reached Madīnah. May Allāh reward him for his good companionship.

I then entered upon Umm Salamah while I was veiled, she did not recognize me until I introduced myself and uncovered my veil. She embraced me and said: 'You have emmigrated to Allāh, Mighty and Majestic, and to His Messenger?' I said: 'Yes, but I fear that he will send me back just as he sent back Abū Jandal and Abū Baṣīr, and the situation of men is not like that of women. My people will search for me, I have been away for five days now since I left them. They will estimate the amount of time I usually stayed away, then they will search for me. If they do not find me, they will set out.'”

Then the Messenger of Allāh ﷺ entered upon Umm Salamah, and she informed him of the news of Umm Kulthūm. He welcomed and reassured her.

I said: "I have fled to you with my religion. Keep me and do not send me back to them, for they trial and torture me. I have no patience for torture. I am only a woman—weak as women are, as you know. And I have seen that you sent back two men until one of them refused."
He said: "Indeed, Allāh, Mighty and Majestic, has nullified the treaty regarding women and has made a ruling concerning them with which they are all pleased." As he used to send back the women.

The next day, her two brothers—Al-Walīd and ʿAmmārah—came and said: "Fulfill your promise and the covenant you made with us."
He said: "Allāh has nullified the treaty." So they left.
                                                           (1/2)

messages.from: عَلَىٰ نَهجِ السَّلَفِ || Upon the path of the Salaf 📚
text:
I (Ibn al-Jawzī) said: I know that the annulment of the treaty regarding women refers to the revelation of the test concerning their rights. So the Messenger of Allāh ﷺ tested her, and tested the women after her. He would say to them: "By Allāh, nothing caused you to leave except love for Allāh, His Messenger, and ʾIslām. You did not leave for the sake of a husband or wealth." When they said that, he would keep them and not send them back to their families.

Umm Kulthūm was a young unmarried woman at that time, so Zayd ibn Ḥārithah married her. After he was killed, she married Az-Zubayr, and she bore him Zaynab. Then she married ʿAbdurRaḥmān ibn ʿAwf, and she bore him Ibrāhīm and Ḥumayd. Then she married ʿAmr ibn al-ʿĀṣ, and she died in his care, may Allāh have mercy on her.
                                                           (2/2)

text.text:
Ṣifat aṣ-Ṣafwah by Ibn al-Jawzī (1/322–323)
Related: Ṣaḥīḥ al-Bukhārī (2711)

text: Tr: Umm Tamīm aṣ-Ṣūmāliyyah

text.text:
يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِذَا جَاءَكُمُ الْمُؤْمِنَاتُ مُهَاجِرَاتٍ فَامْتَحِنُوهُنَّ ۖ اللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِإِيمَانِهِنَّ ۖ فَإِنْ عَلِمْتُمُوهُنَّ مُؤْمِنَاتٍ فَلَا تَرْجِعُوهُنَّ إِلَى الْكُفَّارِ ۖ لَا هُنَّ حِلٌّ لَّهُمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحِلُّونَ لَهُنَّ ۖ وَآتُوهُم مَّا أَنفَقُوا ۚ وَلَا جُنَاحَ عَلَيْكُمْ أَن تَنكِحُوهُنَّ إِذَا آتَيْتُمُوهُنَّ أُجُورَهُنَّ ۚ وَلَا تُمْسِكُوا بِعِصَمِ الْكَوَافِرِ وَاسْأَلُوا مَا أَنفَقْتُمْ وَلْيَسْأَلُوا مَا أَنفَقُوا ۚ ذَٰلِكُمْ حُكْمُ اللَّهِ ۖ يَحْكُمُ بَيْنَكُمْ ۚ وَاللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ 

"O you who believe! When believing women come to you as emigrants, examine them; Allāh knows best as to their Faith, then if you ascertain that they are true believers send them not back to the disbelievers. They are not lawful (wives) for the disbelievers nor are the disbelievers lawful (husbands) for them. But give them (the disbelievers) that (amount of money) which they have spent [as their Mahr] to them. And there will be no sin on you to marry them if you have paid their Mahr to them. Likewise hold not the disbelieving women as wives, and ask for (the return of) that which you have spent (as Mahr ) and let them (the disbelievers) ask back for that which they have spent. That is the Judgement of Allāh, He judges between you. And Allāh is All-Knowing, All-Wise." [Al-Mumtaḥanah: 10]
Tr: Hilālī & Khān

text.text: Important advice for sisters attending lectures via livestreams

text: Some sisters, as mentioned

text.text: here

text:
, may recieve messages from men due to their accounts being easily found in livestream lists. The goal of this post isn't only to avoid receiving messages, rather the goal is to avoid fitnah and close any doors of evil.

I have attached visual steps on how to make a public channel to use when joining livestreams in order to keep your account private and to preserve your modesty بإذن الله.

1️⃣ Make a new channel, not a group.

2️⃣ Name it something plain and simple, a mere dot could suffice. I advise our sisters not to beautify this channel and to strive to keep it as unassuming as possible.

3️⃣ Make sure the channel is public, as if it is private you will not be able to attend livestreams with it. As for the channel link, it can simply be a mix of random letters. 

Remember, the goal is to attend the livestreams privately and without drawing attention to the channel to avoid fitnah.

text.text: How do I join with my new channel?

text:
In the corner of any livestream you join there will be three dots in the corner, then you will see a button that says "Display me as.."
Press on it, and choose the new public channel you made.
Note: You shouldn't have to do this every single time, but make sure to check if you're displayed as your private account or your public channel often just in case.

We ask Allāh to grant the Muslim women modesty and ḥayāʾ in all their affairs

text_entities.text: Important advice for sisters attending lectures via livestreams
Some sisters, as mentioned
here

, may recieve messages from men due to their accounts being easily found in livestream lists. The goal of this post isn't only to avoid receiving messages, rather the goal is to avoid fitnah and close any doors of evil.

I have attached visual steps on how to make a public channel to use when joining livestreams in order to keep your account private and to preserve your modesty بإذن الله.

1️⃣ Make a new channel, not a group.

2️⃣ Name it something plain and simple, a mere dot could suffice. I advise our sisters not to beautify this channel and to strive to keep it as unassuming as possible.

3️⃣ Make sure the channel is public, as if it is private you will not be able to attend livestreams with it. As for the channel link, it can simply be a mix of random letters. 

Remember, the goal is to attend the livestreams privately and without drawing attention to the channel to avoid fitnah.
How do I join with my new channel?

In the corner of any livestream you join there will be three dots in the corner, then you will see a button that says "Display me as.."
Press on it, and choose the new public channel you made.
Note: You shouldn't have to do this every single time, but make sure to check if you're displayed as your private account or your public channel often just in case.

We ask Allāh to grant the Muslim women modesty and ḥayāʾ in all their affairs

text: From the virtues of

text.text: Khadījah bint Khuwaylid

text:
, may Allāh be pleased with her.

بِـسم اللّٰـه الرّحمـٰن الرّحيـم

“She never compelled him to raise his voice” So take Khadījah—may Allāh be pleased with her—as your example, O women!

text.text: Abū Hurayrah (may Allāh be pleased with him), narrated: Jibrīl came to the Prophet ﷺ and said: “O Messenger of Allāh! This is Khadījah, she has come to you carrying a vessel containing condiments and food. When she comes to you, convey to her greetings of peace from her Lord and from me, and give her glad tidings of a house in Paradise made of hollowed pearls (qaṣab), wherein there is no clamour (ṣakhab) nor fatigue (naṣab).”  Agreed upon.

text: It is mentioned in Fatḥ al-Bārī (11/134):

text.text: “Made of qaṣab”

text:
: Meaning a hollow pearl, spacious like a lofty palace.

As-Suhaylī said: “The subtle point  in the wording “qaṣab” instead of “pearl” is that it corresponds to her having attained the lead (qaṣab al-sabaq) by hastening to faith before others. This is why this connection appears throughout this entire ḥadīth." [End quote]

There is another connection in “qaṣab” as most of its tubes are straight and even—likewise Khadījah had a levelness not found in others. She was keen upon pleasing him ﷺ in every possible way, and nothing ever came from her that angered him, unlike others.

text.text: “A house”

text: : Meaning a house in addition to what Allāh prepared for her as the reward of her deeds. Hence he said:

text.text: “nor fatigue”

text:
, meaning she did not experience hardship in attaining it.

As-Suhaylī said: "Mentioning "house" has a subtle meaning, because she was a mistress of a house before the Prophethood, then she became the mistress of a house of ʾIslām alone! As there was no house of ʾIslām on earth at the first day of the Prophet’s ﷺ prophethood except her house—this is a virtue no one else shared."
He added: "Reward is often mentioned using the same expression as the deed, even if the reward is more noble. Hence the ḥadīth says “house” rather than “palace.”" [End quote]

And in mentioning the “house” lies another meaning, reflecting that the Prophet’s household [children] ultimately returned to her.

text.text: “Wherein there is no ṣakhab nor naṣab”
Ṣakhab

text: : Arguments and dispute with raised voices.

text.text: Naṣab

text:
: Fatigue.

As-Suhaylī said: “The suitability of negating these two is that when the Prophet ﷺ called her to ʾIslām, she responded willingly—she did not make him raise his voice, nor dispute, nor struggle. Rather, she removed from him all hardship, made him feel at ease from every loneliness, and made every difficulty easy for him. So it was fitting that her dwelling, with which her Lord gave her glad tidings, be described with the qualities corresponding with her actions.” (End of quote, abridged)

———•———•———•———•———

In ‘Umdat al-Qārī (16/281):
“..The mention of “clamour” and “fatigue” is also by way of correspondence; as when he ﷺ called her to faith, she responded quickly and did not require him to raise his voice as a man might if his wife resisted him, nor did she cause him hardship. Rather, she removed every hardship from him, made him feel at ease from every loneliness, eased every difficulty, and relieved burdens with her wealth. Thus her abode was described in a way that corresponds to her state and actions.” [End quote]

———•———•———•———•———

In Tafsīr ibn Kathīr (6/404):
“From the special qualities of Khadījah (may Allāh be pleased with her) is that she never upset him, never angered him, and he never had to take an oath of abstinence from her, nor was there ever any blame or abandonment between them. And this alone is a sufficient virtue and merit.” [End quote]

So may Allāh be pleased with our mother Khadījah and all the Mothers of the Believers, and gather us with them in the highest Paradise. Indeed, my Lord is All-Hearing, All-Knowing.

text.text: Written by: Ḥassānah bint Muḥammad al-ʾAlbānī حفظها الله

text: Tr: Umm Tamīm aṣ-Ṣūmāliyyah

text.text: What Are the Means That Save One from the Torment of the Grave?
Ibn al-Qayyim

text:
رحمه الله says: Its answer is also from two aspects: a general one and a detailed one.

As for the general:

text.text: It is to avoid those causes that bring about the punishment of the grave.

text: Among the most beneficial of them is that a person, when he intends to sleep, sits for an hour for the sake of Allāh, in which he takes account of himself regarding what he has lost and gained during his day. Then he renews for himself a sincere repentance between him and Allāh and sleeps upon that repentance, resolving not to return to the sin when he wakes up.

text.text: He does this every night.

text:
If he dies that night, he dies upon repentance; and if he wakes, he wakes ready for action [i.e. acts of worship], delighted that his time has been delayed so that he may meet his Lord as well as make up what he has missed.

There is nothing more beneficial for a servant than this sleep—especially if he follows it with remembrance of Allāh and practicing the Sunan reported from the Messenger of Allāh ﷺ at the time of sleep, until sleep overtakes him. For whoever Allāh intends good for, He grants him success in that. And there is no power except with Allāh.

Source: Ar-Rūḥ (p.79 – ط العلمية)
(p. 231 – ط عطاءات العلم)
Tr: Umm Tamīm aṣ-Ṣūmāliyyah

text.text: Stop complaining to the people!

As Sufyān Ath-Thawrī said:

حَدَّثَنَا أَحْمَدُ، نَا إِبْرَاهِيمُ الحَرْبِيُ، نَا أَبُو حَذَيْفَةَ؛ قَالَ: رَأَى الثَّوْرِيِّ رَجُلًا عِنْدَ قَوْمٍ يَشْكُو ضِيقَهُ، فَقَالَ لَهُ الثَّوْرِيُّ: يَا هَذَا! شَكَوْتَ مَنْ يَرْحَمُكَ إِلَى مَنْ لا يَرْحَمُك!

Aḥmed reported that Ibrāhīm Al-Ḥarbī narrated that Abū Ḥudhayfah said, "Ath-Thawrī saw a man sitting with people complaining about his difficult situation, and he said to him, 'Hey you! You complained about the One who has mercy for you to the ones who do not have mercy for you."

[al-Mujālasah wa Jawāhir al-ʿIlm (al-Dīnawarī)]

text:
The One putting you through these difficulties is the One who has Mercy for you. 

The hardships He puts you through make you to return to Him — this is in itself a mercy.

So do not be from those who respond to trials by turning to the people with complaints.
Because in reality, such a person is complaining about

text.text: the One who put him in this tribulation

text:
.

And this is the reality every time we complain.

 ﴿ قَالَ إِنَّمَاۤ أَشۡكُوا۟ بَثِّی وَحُزۡنِیۤ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ ﴾

text.text: “He said: I only complain of my grief and my sorrow to Allāh”

text:
[Yūsuf, 86]

Be like Prophet Yaʿqūb عليه السلام. Humble yourself before your Rabb and take your complaints to the only One who can remove your difficulty.

People do not carry with them the solutions to your problems.

What can the people do for you when they have problems of their own? How will the people help you if Allāh does not will for them to help you? How can you rely on the people to save you when they are just as poor as you?

﴿ یَـٰۤأَیُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ أَنتُمُ ٱلۡفُقَرَاۤءُ إِلَى ٱللَّهِۖ وَٱللَّهُ هُوَ ٱلۡغَنِیُّ ٱلۡحَمِیدُ ﴾

text.text: “O humanity! It is you who are poor and in need of Allāh, but Allāh is Rich (free of all needs), Worthy of all praise.”

text:
[Fāṭir, 15]

So turn your heart to Allāh ﷻ — not the people. For the One who trials you is the only One who can relieve you.

—Benefitted from Ustādh Muḥammad ibn James Sutton وفقه الله 

🖊️Umm Muṣʿab Al-Ghāniyyah Al-Almāniyyah

text.text: To the mothers seeking knowledge..

text:
Your pursuit of knowledge will be interrupted by your little child’s crying, by his hand reaching for something trivial in your eyes, by his clinging to your embrace as if it were his only homeland, and by his insistence that you sit beside him for no reason except to see you. He will ask you to applaud a scribble, praise an idea, and listen to endless talk.

But remember—amid all of this—these are moments that will never return. This little one is not competing with your path; rather, he walks along it with you in his innocent way.

He does not know knowledge as you know it, but he knows you. He sees you as his world, his safety, and the most beautiful part of his day.

So do not misunderstand him. He is not hindering your journey; he is refining your heart and nurturing in you a mercy that purifies your knowledge and blesses your striving.

And know, O woman of determination, that he sees you. He stores in his memory your time with your book, your leaning over your notebook, your persistence despite his interruptions. A day will come when he imitates you without you even realizing it, combining what he saw of your love for him with what he saw of your sincerity in seeking knowledge.

text.text: So balance

text: Do not neglect his heart for the sake of your knowledge, and do not abandon your knowledge for the sake of his moments. Let them meet within you—so you become a mother who nurtures and a seeker who rises.

Written by a sister

Forwarded and translated by:
Umm ʿAbdirraḥmān as-Sūdāniyyah

text.text: Not Every Interruption Is An Interruption

text:
So there she sits with her book…finally, after a long day, she finally finds a quiet moment. A page opens, her mind slowly settles, and ideas begin connecting.

But then:

"Ummi…"

A scribb…sorry, a piece of art…needs admiration.
A button that needs "fixing".
A story that goes nowhere needs an ear, right now!
Or if they feel like being honest, then no reason except that they want her near…now the button doesn't need fixing.

She perhaps feels pulled away from something "more valuable" (you heard that right).

Because these moments feel small while they are happening.

A question.
A scribb…sorry, again, a piece of art.
A child asking her to watch something unimportant (how dare you) for the third time that day.


And when things repeat often enough, the nafs slowly begins treating them as background noise instead of something weighty.

And perhaps that is the danger.

That these moments feel so small...that a mother can begin seeing them as obstacles standing between her and what she wanted to finish.

But yā ukhti, childhood does not remain in this form for long.

A child who keeps pulling you away today will not always do so.

And this child is not trying to stand between you and knowledge.

He only wants his mommy.

He does not know what masʾalah you are studying.
He does not understand what benefit made you pause and reflect.
He cannot distinguish between tafsīr, fiqh, and ʿaqīdah.

But he understands something else very clearly.

He understands whether your presence feels gentle…in those moments.
Whether he feels welcomed near you…in those moments.
Whether knowledge made the atmosphere of the home colder…or warmer.

This is something many mothers do not notice: the child is not only watching that you study, he is watching how you treat him while you study.

He sees whether knowledge made you harsher…or gentler.

Whether your book always feels like something that takes you away from him…or something that still leaves room for warmth beside it.

So even these interruptions are part of his education, and your education.

Because before a child loves knowledge itself, he first learns how knowledge feels through the one carrying it.

And that knowledge you're seeking, is just a means to worship Allāh correctly.

Among the most important acts of worship for a woman, is, taking care of her children.

So, letting knowledge seeking take more priority than your child defeats the purpose.

Think of it as overeating. The point is health, not food itself.

Now back again to your child:

Your child feels the atmosphere while those books are present. 

Warm? Cold? Harsh? Gentle?

These early feelings settle deep.

Years later, he may not remember the exact books you read.

But he will remember the atmosphere surrounding them.

The woman who kept learning…without making him feel abandoned by her learning.

The woman whose pursuit of knowledge did not erase softness from the home.

So balance.

Do not let your child feel that knowledge stole you from him.

And do not let the light of knowledge disappear from the home entirely.

Let your child grow up seeing that knowledge and mercy were never enemies.

That the same hands which held the book…still held him gently too. 🌿

— Abū Sahl, ʿAbdurraḥmān bnu Mīhūb al-Qaddārī al-Jazāʾirī

text_entities.text: Not Every Interruption Is An Interruption

So there she sits with her book…finally, after a long day, she finally finds a quiet moment. A page opens, her mind slowly settles, and ideas begin connecting.

But then:

"Ummi…"

A scribb…sorry, a piece of art…needs admiration.
A button that needs "fixing".
A story that goes nowhere needs an ear, right now!
Or if they feel like being honest, then no reason except that they want her near…now the button doesn't need fixing.

She perhaps feels pulled away from something "more valuable" (you heard that right).

Because these moments feel small while they are happening.

A question.
A scribb…sorry, again, a piece of art.
A child asking her to watch something unimportant (how dare you) for the third time that day.


And when things repeat often enough, the nafs slowly begins treating them as background noise instead of something weighty.

And perhaps that is the danger.

That these moments feel so small...that a mother can begin seeing them as obstacles standing between her and what she wanted to finish.

But yā ukhti, childhood does not remain in this form for long.

A child who keeps pulling you away today will not always do so.

And this child is not trying to stand between you and knowledge.

He only wants his mommy.

He does not know what masʾalah you are studying.
He does not understand what benefit made you pause and reflect.
He cannot distinguish between tafsīr, fiqh, and ʿaqīdah.

But he understands something else very clearly.

He understands whether your presence feels gentle…in those moments.
Whether he feels welcomed near you…in those moments.
Whether knowledge made the atmosphere of the home colder…or warmer.

This is something many mothers do not notice: the child is not only watching that you study, he is watching how you treat him while you study.

He sees whether knowledge made you harsher…or gentler.

Whether your book always feels like something that takes you away from him…or something that still leaves room for warmth beside it.

So even these interruptions are part of his education, and your education.

Because before a child loves knowledge itself, he first learns how knowledge feels through the one carrying it.

And that knowledge you're seeking, is just a means to worship Allāh correctly.

Among the most important acts of worship for a woman, is, taking care of her children.

So, letting knowledge seeking take more priority than your child defeats the purpose.

Think of it as overeating. The point is health, not food itself.

Now back again to your child:

Your child feels the atmosphere while those books are present. 

Warm? Cold? Harsh? Gentle?

These early feelings settle deep.

Years later, he may not remember the exact books you read.

But he will remember the atmosphere surrounding them.

The woman who kept learning…without making him feel abandoned by her learning.

The woman whose pursuit of knowledge did not erase softness from the home.

So balance.

Do not let your child feel that knowledge stole you from him.

And do not let the light of knowledge disappear from the home entirely.

Let your child grow up seeing that knowledge and mercy were never enemies.

That the same hands which held the book…still held him gently too. 🌿

— Abū Sahl, ʿAbdurraḥmān bnu Mīhūb al-Qaddārī al-Jazāʾirī

messages.from: عَلَىٰ نَهجِ السَّلَفِ || Upon the path of the Salaf 📚

text.text: Thought Process Behind The Rewrite Above

text:
One thing I noticed while rewriting the original piece is that the problem was not the ideas themselves.

The ideas are actually good.

The problem was the movement between them.

Writing quality is not just vocabulary, emotional lines, metaphors, or sentence beauty.

But a huge part of strong writing is structural flow.

Meaning: why does this paragraph come after that one?

The original piece had multiple good ideas:

    children interrupting the mother

    these moments being temporary

    the child loving the mother

    motherhood refining mercy

    the child later admiring knowledge

    balance

But the piece kept changing centers abruptly.

For example, it went from: "the child loves his mother", to: "motherhood refines the woman spiritually".

Then suddenly: "the child later admires knowledge."

Those are not naturally connected yet.

They are individually good ideas, but the transitions are weak.

So instead of adding more lines, I tried identifying the actual spine of the piece.

The stronger center became: The child experiences knowledge through the mother carrying it.

That single idea reorganized the structure.

Now the interruptions themselves become important.

Why?

Because the child is not merely seeing that his mother studies.

He is seeing:

    how she reacts while studying

    whether knowledge made her gentle or harsh

    whether the book emotionally pushes him away

    or whether warmth still exists beside it

Now the paragraphs connect naturally.

The interruptions lead into emotional atmosphere.

The atmosphere leads into the child's perception of knowledge.

That perception leads into how he may later love knowledge himself.

Then another issue appeared.

The piece still treated knowledge as though it were separate. Which is the core cause of this whole topic.

So the hierarchy itself needed correction.

Knowledge is a means.

The goal is worshipping Allāh correctly.

And among the worship of a woman is properly caring for her children.

So if knowledge-seeking causes neglect of the child, then the means started damaging the objective itself.

That is why I added the overeating analogy.

Food is good.
Health is the goal.

Likewise:
Knowledge is good.
Correct worship is the goal.

That change strengthened the entire ending because now the conclusion comes from principles.

A better form of writing avoids stacking ideas beside each other instead of making one idea produce the next one naturally.

The reader should feel carried forward gradually, not teleported between paragraphs.

Like a gradient. 🌿

Thank you for the idea, it bloomed in someone's mind. 🌸

— Abū Sahl, ʿAbdurraḥmān bnu Mīhūb al-Qaddārī al-Jazāʾirī

text.text: Precious Glass

عَنْ أَنَسٍ أَنَّ النَّبِيَّ صَلَّى اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ أَتَى عَلَى أَزْوَاجِهِ وَسَوَّاقٌ يَسُوقُ بِهِنَّ يُقَالُ لَهُ أَنْجَشَةُ فَقَالَ:
«وَيْحَكَ يَا أَنْجَشَةُ، رُوَيْدًا سَوْقَكَ بِالْقَوَارِيرِ»

Anas ibn Mālik reported that the Prophet ﷺ came upon his wives while they were being led by a camel-driver named Anjashah. The Prophet ﷺ said:

“O Anjashah, be gentle when carrying the delicate glass vessels.”

[ṣaḥīḥ al-bukhārī 6161, ṣaḥīḥ muslim 2323]

text:
My dear sisters,

You have a walī for a reason.

We, as women, are naturally gentle, soft, and tender-hearted.
Valuable and precious, yet easily affected.
We are trusting, empathetic, and inclined to think well of others.
Our hearts are delicate and deserve to be handled with care.

And through our beautiful dīn, we are concealed, honoured, and protected.

Access to you is not for everyone.
It is for the one who proves himself worthy — the one who passes the rigorous tests.

So honour yourself, my dear sister.

Do not allow just anyone into the private spaces of your heart before the marriage contract (عقد) is complete.
Do not give your softness and your vulnerability to one who is still a stranger to you in the sight of Allāh.

For once glass is shattered, it can never fully return to its original state —
even if the one who broke it had good intentions,
even if the damage was accidental.

So let communication pass through a third person,
and protect yourself from unnecessary heartbreak.

Prevention is better than cure.

Be mindful of the attachments you allow to settle within your heart.
Do not underestimate their effects, nor assume yourself immune to harm,
for our Creator knows our nature best.

- أختكن أم مصعب الغانية الألمانية

text.text: 📌 The Virtue of the First Ten Days of Dhul-Ḥijjah

text:
• Al-Ḥāfiẓ Ibn Rajab رحمه الله said:
“Indeed the month of Dhul-Ḥijjah is the best of the sacred months, as it is the greatest of them in sanctity.”

text.text: Fatḥ al-Bārī (20/9)

text:
• Imām Ibn al-Qayyim رحمه الله said:
“The best deed during the ten days of Dhul-Ḥijjah is to increase in acts of worship—especially takbīr (saying Allāhu akbar), tahlīl (lā ilāha illallāh), and taḥmīd (Alḥamdulillāh). This is better than jihād that is not individually obligatory.”

text.text: Madārij as-Sālikīn (1/110)

text:
• Al-Ḥāfiẓ Ibn Rajab رحمه الله said:
“As for the voluntary deeds in the ten days of Dhul-Ḥijjah, they are better than the voluntary deeds in the last ten nights of Ramaḍān. Likewise, the obligatory deeds in the ten of Dhul-Ḥijjah are multiplied more than obligatory deeds in other times.”

text.text: Fatḥ al-Bārī (9/16)

text:
• Al-Ḥāfiẓ Ibn Ḥajar رحمه الله said:
“What appears to be the reason for the distinction of the ten days of Dhul-Ḥijjah is that the major acts of worship are all gathered in them: prayer, fasting, charity, and Ḥajj—and this does not occur at any other time.”

text.text: Fath al-Bārī (2/460)

text:
• Shaykh al-Islām Ibn Taymiyyah رحمه الله said:
“Spending the ten days of Dhul-Ḥijjah fully in worship—night and day—is better than jihād in which one does not sacrifice his life and wealth. Worship at other times equals jihād due to the authentic well-known narrations.”

text.text: Al-Fatāwā al-Kubrā (5/343)

text: Tr: Umm ʿAbdirraḥmān as-Sūdāniyyah

messages.from: عَلَىٰ نَهجِ السَّلَفِ || Upon the path of the Salaf 📚
text: It is recorded in the Ṣaḥīḥayn [al-Bukhārī 45, Muslim 3017] on the authority of ʿUmar ibn al-Khaṭṭāb رضي الله عنه that a man from among the Jews said to him: "Yā ʾAmīr al-Muʾminīn , there is an āyah in your Book which, had it been revealed to us —the assembly of Jews— we would have taken that day as a day of celebration (ʿĪd)." ʿUmar asked: "Which āyah?" The man replied:

text.text:
​{الْيَوْمَ أَكْمَلْتُ لَكُمْ دِينَكُمْ وَأَتْمَمْتُ عَلَيْكُمْ نِعْمَتِي وَرَضِيتُ لَكُمُ الْأِسْلامَ دِيناً} [المائدة: ٣]
{This day I have perfected for you your religion and completed My favour upon you and have approved for you Islām as religion} [Al-Māʾidah: 3]

text:
﻿
ʿUmar then said: "I surely know the day on which it was revealed and the place where it was revealed; it was revealed while the Messenger of Allāh ﷺ was standing at ʿArafah on a Friday."

text.text: A beautiful benefit regarding this āyah:

text:
The perfection of the religion that day was achieved through many aspects, among them:

• The Muslims had not performed the Ḥajj of Islām after Ḥajj was made obligatory prior to that, not any one of them — this is the view of the majority or many of the scholars. Thus, their religion was perfected by completing the practice of all the pillars of Islām.

• Allāh تعالى restored the Ḥajj to the foundations laid by Ibrāhīm عليه السلام and eradicated shirk and it's people; thus, not a single one of them mixed with the Muslims at that standing site. Ash-Shaʿbī said: "This āyah was revealed to the Prophet ﷺ while he was standing at ʿArafah, when he stood where Ibrāhīm had stood, when shirk had vanished, the landmarks of the pre-islāmic ignorance (Jāhiliyyah) were demolished, and no one circumambulated the House (al-Kaʿba) naked." Qatādah and others said the same. It was also said: "No āyah permitting or prohibiting anything (Ḥalāl or Ḥarām) was revealed after it." This was stated by Abū Bakr ibn ʿAyyāsh.

text.text: Benefited from: Laṭāʾif al-Maʿārif by Ibn Rajab رحمه الله chapter regarding the day of ʿArafah and ʿĪd an-Naḥr

text: Tr: Umm ʿAbdirraḥmān as-Sūdāniyyah

text_entities.text: It is recorded in the Ṣaḥīḥayn [al-Bukhārī 45, Muslim 3017] on the authority of ʿUmar ibn al-Khaṭṭāb رضي الله عنه that a man from among the Jews said to him: "Yā ʾAmīr al-Muʾminīn , there is an āyah in your Book which, had it been revealed to us —the assembly of Jews— we would have taken that day as a day of celebration (ʿĪd)." ʿUmar asked: "Which āyah?" The man replied:

​{الْيَوْمَ أَكْمَلْتُ لَكُمْ دِينَكُمْ وَأَتْمَمْتُ عَلَيْكُمْ نِعْمَتِي وَرَضِيتُ لَكُمُ الْأِسْلامَ دِيناً} [المائدة: ٣]
{This day I have perfected for you your religion and completed My favour upon you and have approved for you Islām as religion} [Al-Māʾidah: 3]

﻿
ʿUmar then said: "I surely know the day on which it was revealed and the place where it was revealed; it was revealed while the Messenger of Allāh ﷺ was standing at ʿArafah on a Friday."
A beautiful benefit regarding this āyah:

The perfection of the religion that day was achieved through many aspects, among them:

• The Muslims had not performed the Ḥajj of Islām after Ḥajj was made obligatory prior to that, not any one of them — this is the view of the majority or many of the scholars. Thus, their religion was perfected by completing the practice of all the pillars of Islām.

• Allāh تعالى restored the Ḥajj to the foundations laid by Ibrāhīm عليه السلام and eradicated shirk and it's people; thus, not a single one of them mixed with the Muslims at that standing site. Ash-Shaʿbī said: "This āyah was revealed to the Prophet ﷺ while he was standing at ʿArafah, when he stood where Ibrāhīm had stood, when shirk had vanished, the landmarks of the pre-islāmic ignorance (Jāhiliyyah) were demolished, and no one circumambulated the House (al-Kaʿba) naked." Qatādah and others said the same. It was also said: "No āyah permitting or prohibiting anything (Ḥalāl or Ḥarām) was revealed after it." This was stated by Abū Bakr ibn ʿAyyāsh.
Benefited from: Laṭāʾif al-Maʿārif by Ibn Rajab رحمه الله chapter regarding the day of ʿArafah and ʿĪd an-Naḥr
Tr: Umm ʿAbdirraḥmān as-Sūdāniyyah
